key_insight: Natural fractures redirect hydraulic fractures by locally redistributing stress, so HF paths depend on whether shear-induced stress changes make the fracture zone more compressive or easier to open.
engineering_relevance: Predicting and controlling fracture trajectory in subsurface stimulation and storage requires accounting for NF orientation, local stress redistribution, and mixed-mode fracture behavior.
method: Coupled laboratory fracture tests on intact and pre-fractured PMMA under plane-strain conditions
Digital image correlation to measure full-field displacement and strain during loading and injection
Poroelastic extended finite element simulations to reproduce HF-NF interactions and fracture curvature
evidence_signal: The study compared experimental observations with numerical reproduction of HF trajectories, showing that intact specimens followed pure Mode I while pre-fractured specimens required mixed-mode criteria to match curved propagation.
watch_item: Results are based on PMMA laboratory specimens and plane-strain loading, so field-scale rock heterogeneity and 3D fracture effects may alter the observed trajectories.
paper_title: Local Stress Redistribution Controls Interactions between Hydraulic Fractures and Pre-existing Fractures
